description: Workflow DSL schema used by LLMs and agents to assemble LangGraph workflows.

nodes.description: Workflow nodes. Each node instantiates a registered capability.

edges.description: Unconditional connections. Edges must not contain logic or conditions.

routes.description: Branch mappings from route key to target node id.

entry_point.description: Node id where graph execution starts.

id.description: Unique node id within the workflow.

capability.description: Registered capability name from capability catalog.

config.description: Capability-specific configuration. Resource references such as prompt_ref, script_ref, and spec_ref may appear here when supported by a capability.

policies.description: Optional node policies. Policies are not graph nodes.

capability.description: Registered policy capability name from capability catalog.

edge.description: Two-item array: [source node id, target node id].

from.description: Source flow node id that writes an internal route key.

branches.description: Object mapping route key strings to target node ids.

constraints: Top-level DSL fields are nodes, edges, routes, and entry_point.
Node ids must be unique.
Node capability must exist in the capability catalog and runtime registry.
Policy capability must exist in the capability catalog and policy registry.
Edges are always two-item arrays and must not contain condition fields.
Conditional logic belongs in flow.* nodes.
Routes map route keys to target nodes.
Resource references are capability config values, not top-level DSL fields.

Use subgraph.parallel for fixed-pattern parallel execution of independent steps; each parallel step writes only output_path into its own result_path and should organize context explicitly through state, config, resource refs, and files. fail_strategy may be fail_fast or collect.

Use subgraph.agent_loop for engineering agent loops that need explicit iteration state, verification, decision contracts, per-iteration sandbox execution, token budget checks, archives, memory, reports, and control-plane handoff. Authoring DSL AGENT_LOOP lowers to this capability, preserves slot declaration order in config.slot_order, may set token_max, and may set on_max/on_error to block or wait_human.

Use subgraph.workflow for an authoring-time child workflow reference compiled into a complete embedded workflow DSL; authoring references are relative to the current workflow directory and runtime must not resolve workflow source paths.

Use subgraph.validation_sandbox when a workflow must run in candidate work_dir and optional target_dir roots before promoting allowed file changes back on success; promote rejects real roots changed since baseline and includes authorized deletions.
exec.* process cwd defaults to the client workspace root when cwd is omitted.

prompt_ref, script_ref, and spec_ref may use root-aware shape {"root":"workflow|workspace","path":"relative/path"}; explicit resource root wins over ref_root. flow.human_approval prompt_ref is restricted to root=workflow.
ref_root is an optional capability config value with shape {"root":"workflow|workspace","path":"relative/path"}; it is the base for prompt_ref/script_ref/spec_ref when those refs omit root.
exec.codex_prompt may include context_refs as a list of resource references with type=file|dir; client runners resolve them and include absolute paths in the Codex handoff prompt.

exec.codex_prompt may include target_dirs and target_files as lists of analysis target paths, plus target_dirs_path and target_files_path for runtime state lists; target paths are not resource references and may be absolute.
Codex target_dirs and target_files represent user-authorized analysis objects. Do not use context_refs for analysis targets when the target may be outside workflow or workspace roots.
For package-local prompts/scripts/specs that should run against the user workspace, prefer ref_root={"root":"workflow","path":"."} plus path-only resource refs, and omit cwd.
Resource reference paths must be relative client-side paths; runtime passes them through and client runners resolve them under workflow, workspace, ref_root, or effective cwd.
Legacy cwd remains supported for backward compatibility.
Compiler lowering must not contain business judgment.
